title: feedDecoder calls decoder.close() with remaining queued chunks when pause interrupts an inner drain and inputClosed is true
description: When onPause() fires during an await decoder.push() inside the inner drain loop, the inner while exits via `if (this.paused) break`. Control then falls through to `if (this.inputClosed && !this.ended)` at line 378, which calls `decoder.close()` and returns — abandoning any chunks still in chunkQueue. The remaining buffered bytes are never pushed to the decoder, producing a silently truncated decode. This condition requires all three: paused=true (set while awaiting push), chunkQueue.size>0 (more chunks remain), and inputClosed=true.
evidence: Code at lines 362-381 confirms: inner loop breaks on paused=true, then the outer-loop body falls through to the `inputClosed && !this.ended` check at line 378, which calls decoder.close() and returns before the outer while re-checks paused. Any chunks still in chunkQueue are abandoned. The fix is to add `&& this.chunkQueue.size === 0` to the inputClosed guard or move it to the outer loop top after waitForResume.

title: readDecoderEvents has no fallback terminal protocol message if the codec async iterable ends without emitting 'final' or 'error'
description: If the codec's events() iterable is exhausted without ever yielding a 'final' or 'error' event (e.g. a codec bug or an animation iterable that ends after last frame without a final event), readDecoderEvents returns silently. The run() finally block calls finishSession(this.state) — but no decode_final, decode_error, or decode_budget_exceeded message is posted first. The consumer is left waiting for a terminal protocol message that never arrives.
evidence: Code at lines 408-567 shows the for-await loop can fall through to function end if no 'final' or 'error' event is emitted. The run() finally at line 266-271 calls finishSession and disposeActiveDecoder but posts zero protocol messages. Any consumer awaiting decode_final/decode_error/decode_budget_exceeded would hang indefinitely.

title: progressionTarget !== 'final' with emitEveryPass=true silently behaves like progressionTarget='final', ignoring the requested stop point
description: The early-finish guard at line 486 reads: `if (progressionTarget !== 'final' && !emitEveryPass)`. When emitEveryPass=true, this condition is always false — the session never finishes early, even for target='dc'. A caller requesting target='dc' and emitEveryPass=true receives all passes including the final frame, not just DC frames.
evidence: Code at line 486 confirms: the compound condition means emitEveryPass=true suppresses the early-finish for any progressionTarget. This is documented as intentional in CLAUDE.md ratified-decisions ('logic-3 linear curve') but no comment in code explains the interaction. Verdict confirmed as a behavioral quirk with low severity since it is likely intentional design — consumer must not combine non-final target with emitEveryPass=true to get early-stop.

title: adaptiveHwm cache-invalidation threshold uses strict < 1.0, meaning a 1.0 ms EMA drift skips recalculation
description: The cache guard at line 572 is `Math.abs(ema - this._hwmLastEma) < 1.0`. When drift is exactly 1.0 ms the cache is reused (no recalculation). In practice the EMA is continuous and this boundary is rarely hit exactly. Minor impact since HWM only affects drain coalescing.
evidence: Code at line 572 confirmed as described. The strict `< 1.0` means exactly 1.0ms drift keeps stale cache. Impact is negligible since floating-point EMA virtually never lands exactly on 1.0, but the `<= 1.0` semantics would be more correct for the stated 'within 1 ms' invariant.

title: No assertion that a terminal protocol message was sent before finishSession completes
description: The handler has multiple exit paths. It is easy for a new code path to call finishSession without first posting a terminal protocol message (as happens in logic-2 above). Adding a debug-mode invariant that asserts before finishSession would catch this class of bug.
evidence: logic-2 demonstrates that finishSession can be reached without posting a terminal protocol message. The opportunity is genuine — a boolean guard or debug assertion in finishSession would have caught logic-2 at test time. No such guard exists in the current code.

title: No test for pause-with-inputClosed-and-remaining-chunks scenario (the logic-1 truncation bug)
description: The existing pause test only tests pause-and-resume without inputClosed being set during the pause window. There is no test that closes the input while paused mid-drain with chunks remaining — the exact scenario that triggers the premature decoder.close() in logic-1.
evidence: The logic-1 bug is real and there is no test covering the pause+inputClosed+remaining-chunks combination. The test gap is genuine and fixing logic-1 without this test leaves no regression guard.

title: Caller-supplied workerUrl is passed to Worker() without origin or allowlist validation
description: spawnWorker() accepts an arbitrary workerUrl string and passes it directly to new Worker(url, { type: 'module' }). A caller can point the URL at a cross-origin attacker-controlled script.
evidence: Code at line 37 confirms: `const url = workerUrl ?? DEFAULT_WORKER_URL` is passed directly to `new Worker(url, ...)` with no origin validation. The risk is real if workerUrl is sourced from user-controlled input. No defensive check or documentation of caller responsibility exists.

title: Full internal error strings from WASM load failures are forwarded to the main thread
description: When WASM module loading fails, the raw exception message is embedded verbatim in a decode_error message and sent to the main thread. In production these strings may include local file system paths or internal stack traces.
evidence: Code at lines 383-386 confirms verbatim inclusion of `String(err)` in the decode_error message. The same pattern exists for encode at line 467. Whether this is exploitable depends on deployment (local dev vs production CDN), but the pattern is present and unfixed.

title: Raw wasmUrl (caller-supplied) reflected back in thrown error message
description: loadWasmModule() embeds the raw wasmUrl argument in the thrown Error message. This string propagates to the main thread and may contain credentials or internal hostnames if wasmUrl is a signed URL.
evidence: Code at lines 227-232 confirms wasmUrl is included verbatim in the thrown error. This propagates via the worker catch handler (worker.ts line 384) to decode_error.message on the main thread. Risk is low in practice but real for signed CDN URLs.

title: Dimension overflow check uses width*height which can silently truncate for very large integer inputs before comparison
description: The overflow guard computes `width * height` in JavaScript's 64-bit float arithmetic. For values approaching 2^53, the multiplication loses precision before reaching Infinity, potentially producing a result under MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES_GUARD despite representing an impossible allocation. Additionally, the 1 byte/pixel minimum means multi-byte formats (rgba16 at 8 bytes/pixel) can pass the 1 GiB guard while requiring 8 GiB actual allocation.
evidence: Code at lines 419-426 confirmed. The 1-byte/pixel minimum under-estimates for multi-byte formats: rgba16 (8 bytes/pixel) allows a 16384×16384 image (~2 GiB actual) to pass the 1 GiB guard. The per-axis dimension check is also absent. Both are real gaps, though libjxl's own dimension limits constrain practical exploit surface.

title: Diagnostic probe fetch uses the caller-supplied wasmUrl without restricting the URL scheme or host
description: When the dynamic import succeeds but the module lacks createDecoder/createEncoder, loadWasmModule() falls through to a diagnostic HEAD fetch against wasmUrl. A crafted wasmUrl can cause the worker to emit an outbound request to an internal endpoint.
evidence: Code at lines 210-222 confirms: wasmUrl is used directly in a fetch() call with no scheme/host validation. In cloud deployments with SSRF risk (e.g. AWS metadata endpoint), a crafted wasmUrl from a misconfigured caller could trigger unintended outbound requests. The probe is purely diagnostic (result only affects error messages), making it a candidate for removal.

title: Incoming worker messages are not structurally validated before dispatch
description: The onmessage router trusts the shape of every MainToWorkerMessage entirely. A malformed message reaches handler logic and WASM FFI without validation.
evidence: Code at lines 294-335 confirms no runtime shape validation. TypeScript types are erased at runtime; a same-origin page can postMessage arbitrary data to the worker. The attack surface is real though exploitation requires same-origin malicious code.

category: missing origin check on worker messages
title: Worker message handler does not check MessageEvent.origin before processing
description: The MessageEvent.origin field is not inspected before dispatching messages. For DedicatedWorkers this is safe in current browsers, but the absence of the check is a defense-in-depth gap.
evidence: DedicatedWorker spec guarantees that only the owning page can post to it — MessageEvent.origin is always the owner's origin. The finding's own description acknowledges this is safe for DedicatedWorkers. The 'SharedWorker / future spec evolution' scenarios are speculative; this worker is a DedicatedWorker. No actionable real risk.

title: abortedStarts Set grows unboundedly — no eviction for completed sessions
description: Session IDs are added to abortedStarts when a start is cancelled before the WASM loads, and are removed only by abortedStarts.delete() during the successful start path. Rapid create-and-abort cycles with unique IDs cause unbounded growth.
code_excerpt: const abortedStarts = new Set<string>();
evidence: Code at lines 60, 146-148 confirms: `abortedStarts.add(sessionId)` is called from failPendingDecode/failPendingEncode; removal only happens via `abortedStarts.delete(msg.sessionId)` inside the start IIFE success path (lines 379, 396) or doShutdown's `abortedStarts.clear()`. A session that is aborted via failPendingDecode/failPendingEncode never re-enters the start path, so its entry is never deleted. Over a long worker lifetime with many failed/aborted starts this is a real slow leak.

title: onCancel sets cancelled=true then calls finishSession which checks ended, creating a window where a concurrent run() finally path can also call finishSession before cancelled is visible
description: onCancel() sets this.cancelled = true and this.paused = false, posts the metric and protocol message, then calls finishSession('cancelled'). Meanwhile the run() loop's finally block always calls finishSession(this.state). finishSession is guarded by this.ended, so only the first caller wins.
evidence: JS is single-threaded. onCancel() runs as a microtask; finishSession is synchronous and sets this.ended=true before returning. The run() finally's finishSession call is guarded by `if (this.ended) return false` (line 287). There is no actual concurrent execution — the ended guard makes both callers safe. The finder's own analysis reaches the same conclusion but mislabeled it as TOCTOU.

category: unawaited promise / fire-and-forget on error path
title: disposeActiveDecoder() is called with void in failSession/postBudgetExceeded without awaiting, so a subsequent onCancel that also calls disposeActiveDecoder may enter a second dispose race
description: failSession() and postBudgetExceeded() both call `void this.disposeActiveDecoder()`. The idempotent guard (disposePromise !== null) protects the actual dispose call. If decoder.dispose() rejects, the rejection surfaces only to the first awaiter; the void call in failSession eats the rejection completely.
evidence: Code at lines 609-610 and 635-636 confirms void disposeActiveDecoder() in both failSession and postBudgetExceeded. disposeActiveDecoder at line 322 wraps in Promise.resolve().catch(console.error) — rejections from decoder.dispose() are only logged, not surfaced to the session error path. The shared-promise idempotency is correct but rejection information is silently swallowed on the void path.

title: feedEncoder's finish path checks isTerminal() then awaits encoder.finish() without re-checking finished flag after the wait, allowing a concurrent onFinish() to be ignored if it arrives while onFinish was already processed
description: The finder's own analysis concludes this is safe — the double-terminal is handled by finishSession's ended guard, and the timeout closure captures finishTimer correctly. The finish timer fires after Promise.race is already settled, making it harmless.
evidence: The finder's analysis concludes this is safe ('The double-terminal is handled'). Reading the encode-handler run() finally: disposeActiveEncoder is awaited before finishSession, so the ordering is correct. No concrete failure scenario survives scrutiny — the confidence_self_reported=low reflects the finder's own uncertainty.

title: EncodeHandler constructor fires run() with .catch() but the catch calls failSession which itself calls disposeActiveEncoder with void — if encoder.dispose() throws, the rejection is silently swallowed
description: The constructor calls `this.run().catch((err) => this.failSession(...))`. failSession at line 494 fires `void this.disposeActiveEncoder()`. The disposeActiveEncoder IIFE swallows all rejections from both cancel() and dispose() via console.error.
evidence: Code at lines 107-110 and 494 confirmed. disposeActiveEncoder wraps in a Promise that catches all errors with console.error — disposal failures are silently swallowed with no session-level error surfaced. This matches the decode-handler pattern exactly and is a consistent design choice, but the severity=high label is overstated since it only affects error logging quality, not correctness.

title: handleDecodeStart reads hasAnySession synchronously before registering in pendingDecodeStarts, creating a window where a duplicate decode_start for the same sessionId passes the guard
description: The finder's own analysis concludes this is actually safe because the check+set span is synchronous before the first await, and two rapid messages are processed in separate event-loop turns. The epoch counter and pendingDecodeStarts set protect against the race.
evidence: The finder concludes in the description: 'This is actually safe because the entire synchronous preamble (check + set) completes before the first yield.' JS is single-threaded; the check and set at lines 342 and 359 are synchronous. Two decode_start messages arrive in separate macrotask turns, so the second sees pendingDecodeStarts already populated. No real race exists.

title: doShutdown awaits pendingDecodeStarts/pendingEncodeStarts snapshots but new starts registered after the await can be missed, leaking their handlers unfinalised
description: The finder's own analysis concludes: 'This is actually safe because decodeSessions is snapped at the cancel loop iteration time (lines 527-532), not snapped before the await.' Confidence: low.
evidence: Code at lines 518-546 confirmed. shuttingDown=true is set before the await. Any IIFE that had passed the shuttingDown check (read false) before the flag was set will still create a handler; but the cancel loop iterates decodeSessions.values() AFTER the await, so it will see that handler. The finder's own analysis concludes safety. No concrete leak scenario exists.

title: feedDecoder inner loop checks paused flag after await decoder.push() without re-entering the outer loop's waitForResume path, so a resume-then-pause sequence during a push can leave paused=true with the loop not waiting
description: If onPause() fires during decoder.push(), paused is set to true; after push() resumes, the break exits the inner loop. The outer loop then checks `if (this.paused)` and enters waitForResume(). The finder ultimately concludes this is correct; the lost-wake risk only applies if pause/resume events arrive faster than the microtask loop.
evidence: The outer loop at line 350 checks paused and calls waitForResume() every iteration — so after the inner loop breaks on paused=true, the outer loop correctly enters waitForResume(). The wakeResume single-slot concern: onResume() sets paused=false and calls wakeResume(); if the loop is already running (not waiting), the resolve is a no-op — which is correct since paused=false means no wait is needed. No stuck scenario exists.

category: shared mutable state / pre-allocated message objects
title: Pre-allocated _metricMsg/_drainMsg objects are mutated in-place before postMessage — safe per spec comment, but SharedArrayBuffer pixel paths expose the same pattern to a real copy hazard if SAB is ever used for messages
description: The pattern is safe because postMessage() performs a synchronous structured clone. Flagged as a maintenance hazard since the safety invariant is underdocumented.
evidence: The code comment at lines 149-150 explicitly documents the safety invariant ('postMessage() performs a synchronous structured clone before returning'). The current code is correct and the comment is adequate documentation. The hypothetical future-maintainer scenario is too speculative for a confirmed finding.

title: run()'s Promise.all([feedDecoder, readDecoderEvents]) — if readDecoderEvents throws, feedDecoder is abandoned without cancellation, keeping the decoder's async iterator alive indefinitely
description: If readDecoderEvents rejects, feedDecoder continues its loop until the finally block calls disposeActiveDecoder(). finishSession sets this.ended=true and calls wake(), which will break feedDecoder out of its loop after the current await decoder.push() completes. This creates a race between in-flight push() and dispose().
evidence: Code at lines 262-271 confirmed. Promise.all rejects on first rejection; feedDecoder continues with its local decoder reference. The finally's finishSession sets ended=true and calls wake() — but feedDecoder may be mid-await on decoder.push() when disposeActiveDecoder() runs, causing push-after-dispose. The dispose is idempotent via disposePromise guard but push()-on-disposed-decoder behavior depends on the WASM facade and is undefined.

title: run()'s Promise.all([feedEncoder, readEncoderChunks]) has the same abandoned-task hazard as decode-handler — if readEncoderChunks throws, feedEncoder continues pushing pixels to an encoder being disposed
description: Same structural issue as concurrency-9. If readEncoderChunks rejects, feedEncoder continues. The finally calls disposeActiveEncoder, which may run while feedEncoder's in-flight pushPixels is outstanding.
evidence: Code at lines 197-212 confirmed. Same structure as concurrency-9 in decode-handler. Note: encode finally calls disposeActiveEncoder BEFORE finishSession (unlike decode which calls finishSession first) — this ordering means the wake() that breaks feedEncoder's loop hasn't fired yet when disposeActiveEncoder runs, making the push-after-dispose race worse here than in decode.

title: No AbortSignal or cancellation token threads through feedDecoder/readDecoderEvents — cancellation relies on polling this.ended after every await boundary
description: Any new await added inside feedDecoder or readDecoderEvents that does NOT check isTerminal() afterward will become a silent hang point. This is an architecture opportunity, not a current bug.
code_excerpt: private async run(): Promise<void> { ... await Promise.all([this.feedDecoder(decoder), this.readDecoderEvents(decoder)]); ... }
evidence: The polling-ended pattern is confirmed throughout feedDecoder and readDecoderEvents. The concern is real: logic-2 is an example of an exit path that missed posting a terminal message. A systematic cancellation token would make the invariant enforceable. Opportunity is genuine though the fix_difficulty_hint='complex' appropriately flags cost.

title: handleReleaseState cancels handlers with void decode.onCancel() but does not await, so decodeSessions.delete runs before onCancel's disposeActiveDecoder resolves
description: handleReleaseState() calls `void decode.onCancel('release_state').catch(() => undefined)` then immediately calls `decodeSessions.delete(sessionId)`. The delete is intentional for hard teardown, but the old handler's run() loop continues asynchronously until disposeActiveDecoder completes.
evidence: The void+delete pattern is intentional for hard teardown. The onSessionEnd callback problem is separately identified as concurrency-13. The question of whether the old handler's async tail can corrupt state after release_state+new-start depends entirely on concurrency-13's epoch-guard gap. Concurrency-12 alone describes expected behavior; the real bug is concurrency-13.

title: onSessionEnd callback decodeSessions.delete has no epoch guard — a stale handler finishing after release_state+new-start will delete the new handler's map entry
description: The onSessionEnd callback is registered as `(sessionId) => decodeSessions.delete(sessionId)`. If a session is released via handleReleaseState and a new decode_start registers a new handler for the same sessionId, the old handler's run() finally calls onSessionEnd, deleting the new handler's entry from decodeSessions.
evidence: Code at lines 402-406 confirmed: `(sessionId) => decodeSessions.delete(sessionId)` with no identity check. If release_state fires, decodeSessions.delete(sessionId) is called immediately (line 273), then a new decode_start registers a new handler at line 405. When the old handler's finally fires, its onSessionEnd deletes the new entry. Fix: `if (decodeSessions.get(sessionId) === handler) decodeSessions.delete(sessionId)`.

title: onSessionEnd callback encodeSessions.delete has the same no-epoch-guard race as concurrency-13 for encode sessions
description: Identical to concurrency-13 but for the encode session map. The old handler's onSessionEnd fires and deletes the new handler's entry from encodeSessions.
evidence: Code at lines 484-488 confirmed: same pattern as concurrency-13 for encodeSessions. No identity guard. Fix is identical: `if (encodeSessions.get(sessionId) === handler) encodeSessions.delete(sessionId)`.

category: missing test coverage for concurrency scenarios
title: No test covers the release_state + immediate new decode_start race that triggers the onSessionEnd stale-delete bug (concurrency-13/14)
description: No test simulates: session in progress, release_state arrives cancelling it, new decode_start for the same sessionId arrives immediately after, verify new handler receives chunks correctly.
code_excerpt: // No existing test exercises the release_state+new-start scenario
evidence: concurrency-13 and concurrency-14 are confirmed real bugs. A test covering release_state+new-start with same sessionId would catch both as regressions. The test gap is genuine.

title: Exception thrown inside run() finally block can suppress the original error
description: In run(), if feedDecoder or readDecoderEvents throws, the catch calls failSession. Then the finally block calls finishSession() which calls callbacks.onSessionEnd(). If onSessionEnd throws a synchronous exception inside the finally block, that exception propagates out of run() and silently replaces the original error.
evidence: Code at lines 266-271 confirmed. finishSession at line 293 calls `this.callbacks.onSessionEnd(this.sessionId)` without try/catch. Current wiring uses `(sessionId) => decodeSessions.delete(sessionId)` which cannot throw, making the risk latent rather than active. But the type signature does not constrain the callback to be non-throwing, making this a real future-maintenance hazard.

title: Output size guard uses 1 byte/pixel minimum but does not account for multi-byte formats before allocating
description: The guard fires at 1 GiB / 1 byte = 1 billion pixels, which for rgba16 (8 bytes/pixel) is 8 GiB of actual output. A 16384×16384 rgba16 image passes the guard (256 MB < 1 GiB) but the actual WASM heap requirement is 2 GiB.
code_excerpt:
const minOutputBytes = width * height; // minimum 1 byte/pixel (most restrictive)
          if (!Number.isFinite(minOutputBytes) || minOutputBytes > MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES_GUARD) {
evidence: Code at lines 419-421 confirmed. The comment explicitly says '1 byte/pixel (most restrictive)' but this is incorrect: 1 byte/pixel is the least restrictive (any format with more bytes/pixel passes a larger image). The guard should multiply by the bytes-per-pixel for the requested format (available from opts.format). A real crafted codestream could allocate 8× the intended limit for rgba16.

title: run() rejection caught by a lambda that calls failSession, but failSession itself can throw if callbacks.onSessionEnd throws
description: The constructor wires: this.run().catch((err) => this.failSession(...)). Inside failSession, finishSession() is called, which invokes this.callbacks.onSessionEnd(this.sessionId). If that callback throws synchronously, it propagates as an unhandled promise rejection.
code_excerpt: this.run().catch((err: unknown) => this.failSession("Internal", String(err)));
evidence: Code at line 183 confirmed. The .catch handler calls failSession which calls finishSession which calls onSessionEnd (line 293) with no try/catch. Current wiring is safe, but the structural risk of an unhandled rejection from a callback that throws is real and there is no defense.

title: EncodeHandler constructor .catch calls failSession which itself may be in terminal state from run()'s finally, silently losing the error
description: If run() throws after the session was already marked terminal in the finally block, the .catch-level failSession hits the isTerminal() guard and silently returns without posting any error message.
evidence: Code at lines 107-110 confirmed. run()'s finally calls finishSession before returning (line 204). If run() then throws (or rejects) after the finally completes, the .catch calls failSession which hits `if (this.isTerminal()) return` (line 479) silently. The original error is dropped with no logging or reporting.

title: feedDecoder continues calling decoder.push after disposeActiveDecoder may have nulled the decoder reference during concurrent cancel
description: feedDecoder receives a local `decoder` parameter and calls decoder.push() in a loop. Meanwhile, onCancel() calls disposeActiveDecoder() which calls decoder.dispose(). The local reference in feedDecoder remains valid after this.decoder = null, so push() is called on a potentially disposed decoder.
evidence: This is the same root issue as concurrency-9. feedDecoder's local decoder parameter stays alive after disposeActiveDecoder() nulls this.decoder. The cancel sets ended=true and calls wake(), which will eventually exit the outer while loop — but between the wake() call and feedDecoder checking ended, one more push() can execute on the disposed decoder. Confirmed as a real ordering hazard.

title: decoder.close() error in feedDecoder is not caught and will propagate as an unhandled rejection from the Promise.all
description: When inputClosed becomes true, feedDecoder calls `await decoder.close()` with no surrounding try/catch. If decoder.close() throws, the error propagates to run()'s catch which calls failSession. If the session has already terminated, failSession is a no-op and the close error is never surfaced.
evidence: Code at lines 378-381 confirmed: no try/catch around decoder.close(). If close() throws and the session is already terminal (e.g. error event fired from readDecoderEvents), failSession at line 592 returns early via `if (this.ended) return` — the close error is silently swallowed. Low practical impact but the silence is real.

title: width * height multiplication can silently overflow to a float exceeding safe integer range before the Number.isFinite guard
description: For width=2^27, height=2^27, product=2^54 which is still finite but no longer an exact integer. The guard fires when minOutputBytes > 1 GiB = 2^30, so any w*h > 2^30 is caught.
code_excerpt:
const minOutputBytes = width * height; // minimum 1 byte/pixel (most restrictive)
          if (!Number.isFinite(minOutputBytes) || minOutputBytes > MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES_GUARD) {
evidence: The finder acknowledges: 'the guard fires when minOutputBytes > 2^30, so any w*h > 2^30 is caught.' JS float precision loss only matters for values >2^53, which is orders of magnitude above the 2^30 guard threshold. The guard correctly catches all practical overflow cases. The float precision concern is purely theoretical at these dimensions.

title: decoder.close() has no timeout — a hung WASM decoder can stall the feedDecoder loop indefinitely
description: After all chunks are fed and inputClosed is set, feedDecoder calls `await decoder.close()` with no timeout. The encode handler has an explicit 30-second timeout on encoder.finish(). If close() blocks, feedDecoder never returns and the session remains open indefinitely.
evidence: Code at lines 378-381 confirmed: no timeout around decoder.close(). The encode handler implements a 30-second Promise.race timeout on encoder.finish() (lines 354-371 of encode-handler.ts) for exactly this reason. The asymmetry is real and decoder.close() hanging would stall the session indefinitely.

title: WASM load error message is forwarded verbatim to main thread via decode_error, potentially leaking internal paths or stack traces
description: When getWasm() fails, the catch block does: message: `WASM module failed to load: ${String(err)}`. The err value is the raw exception from loadWasmModule which may include file paths or stack frames.
evidence: Duplicate of security-2 (same code, same lines). Both confirmed. The finding is legitimate though severity is low in practice.

category: missing error handling — bare async void call
title: handleReleaseState calls onCancel with void operator but disposeActiveDecoder's internal rejection from decoder.dispose() is silently swallowed
description: In onCancel, disposeActiveDecoder is called with void. If decoder.dispose() rejects, disposeActiveDecoder catches it with console.error only. The rejection is fully orphaned.
evidence: disposeActiveDecoder wraps decoder.dispose() in Promise.resolve().catch(console.error) — all rejections are caught and logged. There is no orphaned rejection; the .catch ensures the returned promise always resolves. The void call discards the resolved promise, but since the catch is internal to disposeActiveDecoder, no unhandled rejection can escape.

category: missing error handling in queue routing
title: flushQueuedDecodeMessages and flushQueuedEncodeMessages propagate any synchronous throw from handler methods, crashing the IIFE
description: flushQueuedDecodeMessages iterates queued messages and calls routeToDecodeHandler. If any synchronous handler method throws (e.g., onChunk's failSession path throws because callbacks.onSessionEnd throws), the exception propagates out of the for-loop. The IIFE has a .catch that re-sends decode_error but the session state may be partially corrupted.
evidence: Code at the flushQueued functions confirmed: no try/catch around the routing loop. A synchronous throw propagates out through the IIFE which does have a .catch (line 408) — but only some queued messages will have been processed, leaving the session in a partial state. The current onSessionEnd implementation cannot throw, making this latent rather than active.

category: missing error boundary — ChunkRing.grow silent OOM
title: ChunkRing.grow() allocates a new Array without catching allocation failure, silently propagating RangeError on OOM
description: grow() does `new Array<ArrayBuffer | undefined>(cap)` where cap doubles each time. In a memory-constrained worker, this can throw a RangeError that propagates to the message handler without a specific session decode_error being sent.
evidence: Code at lines 103-113 confirmed: no try/catch in grow(). A RangeError from `new Array(cap)` would propagate through onChunk() to the message handler, hitting the globalThis 'error' listener which posts worker_error without a sessionId. The specific session never receives a decode_error and the consumer hangs waiting for a terminal message.

title: Error codes are bare strings with no enum/const registry, making exhaustiveness checking and client-side routing impossible
description: failSession is called with code values like 'Internal', 'InvalidInput', 'QueueOverflow', 'CapabilityMissing' — plain string literals scattered across multiple files with no shared const/enum.
evidence: Code at line 584 confirms code is typed as `string`. Callers in worker.ts use string literals 'CapabilityMissing', 'DuplicateSession', 'Internal', 'QueueOverflow', 'InvalidInput' with no shared const. A typo would compile silently. The opportunity is genuine.

title: No shared timeout-race utility leads to ad-hoc timeout patterns: encode has 30s finish timeout, decode has none on close
description: A shared raceWithTimeout(promise, ms, errorMsg) helper would centralize the pattern and make it easy to add the missing decode close timeout.
evidence: errors-8 confirms the missing timeout on decoder.close(). The pattern asymmetry (encode has it, decode doesn't) is real. A shared utility would make the fix more consistent and prevent future omissions.

category: missing error handling — postMessage during shutdown
title: doShutdown() calls Promise.allSettled on pendingDecodeStarts values snapshot but new starts registered after the snapshot are not waited for
description: The finder's own analysis concludes the logic is complex but appears correct because shuttingDown=true prevents new starts and in-flight IIFEs are handled via the cancel loop over decodeSessions.
evidence: As analyzed in concurrency-6: shuttingDown=true blocks all new messages at line 297; any in-flight IIFE that passed the shuttingDown check will register in decodeSessions before the cancel loop runs; the cancel loop iterates decodeSessions live (not a snapshot). No handler can be missed. The finder's confidence_self_reported=low reflects correct uncertainty.

category: missing error handling — test helper waitFor timeout message
title: waitFor timeout message gives no context about which predicate failed, making test failures hard to diagnose
description: The test-only waitFor helper throws 'timed out waiting for handler' with no information about which test or which condition failed.
evidence: The finding describes a real test-quality issue. The message 'timed out waiting for handler' provides no predicate context. Adding predicate.toString() or a caller-supplied label would significantly reduce debug time when tests fail.

category: missing error boundary — wasm-loader probe response body leak
title: Diagnostic probe in loadWasmModule reads response on HEAD 405 but does not cancel or consume the body if cancel() itself throws
description: When the HEAD probe returns 405, the code falls back to a full GET and calls `await resp.body?.cancel()`. If cancel() throws, the outer catch handles it non-fatally. The deeper issue: the fallback GET downloads a potentially large WASM file just to check availability.
code_excerpt:
let resp = await fetchImpl(wasmUrl, { method: "HEAD", signal: ac.signal });
        if (resp.status === 405) { resp = await fetchImpl(wasmUrl, { signal: ac.signal }); await resp.body?.cancel(); }
evidence: Code at lines 216-217 confirmed. The fallback GET has no Content-Length limit, Range header, or size guard. A server returning 405 to HEAD and serving a large WASM file would cause the worker to partially download it just to probe availability. The outer catch makes it non-fatal but wasteful. Low severity.

title: Worker-level error events post worker_error with string-only messages; no structured context (sessionId, stack, timestamp) is included
description: The unhandledrejection and error listeners construct a worker_error message with only a string message field. Adding stack and timestamp would improve post-mortem debugging.
evidence: Code at lines 567-573 confirmed: only message string is forwarded, no stack or timestamp. `event.reason?.stack` is available and would be trivial to add. The opportunity is genuine.

title: Encode handler emits metric names not declared in the CodecMetric discriminated union
description: postFinalMetrics() emits 'encode_create_ms', 'encode_push_pixels_ms', 'encode_wait_pixels_ms', 'encode_finish_ms', 'encode_chunk_yield_ms'. readEncoderChunks() additionally emits 'encode_chunk_yield_total_ms', 'encode_output_bytes', 'encode_time_to_first_byte_ms'. The CodecMetric union only declares 'encode_total_ms'.
evidence: types.ts CodecMetric union (lines 319-341) lists only 'encode_total_ms' from the encode metrics. encode-handler.ts lines 223-227 emit 'encode_create_ms', 'encode_push_pixels_ms', 'encode_wait_pixels_ms', 'encode_finish_ms', 'encode_chunk_yield_ms'. Lines 427, 448-449 emit 'encode_time_to_first_byte_ms', 'encode_chunk_yield_total_ms', 'encode_output_bytes'. None of these are in the union. Any exhaustive switch on CodecMetric.name silently drops them.

title: encode_chunk_yield_ms and encode_chunk_yield_total_ms are both emitted for the same value, creating a duplicate metric
description: readEncoderChunks() posts 'encode_chunk_yield_total_ms' (line 448), then calls postFinalMetrics() which posts 'encode_chunk_yield_ms' (line 227). Both represent the same cumulative chunk-yield duration.
evidence: Code at lines 448 and 227 confirmed: encode_chunk_yield_total_ms (line 448) and encode_chunk_yield_ms (line 227) both carry `this.totalChunkYieldMs` value. Consumers receive both metric names with identical values on every successful encode. One should be removed.

title: partialPixelStride is conditionally omitted when partialPixels is present, violating the protocol contract that declares it required
description: In failSession(), partialPixelStride is only set when the caller passes it as non-undefined. The protocol comment states it is 'required when partialPixels is present'. A new failSession() call with partialPixels but without partialPixelStride would produce a non-compliant MsgDecodeError.
evidence: Code at lines 600-605 confirmed. The protocol contract at protocol.ts declares partialPixelStride 'required when partialPixels is present' but the implementation allows omitting it. Currently all callers either pass it or use the pixelStrideForFormat fallback (error event path at line 561), but the function signature does not enforce this invariant, making future callers a latent contract violation risk.

title: EncodeHandler drain message always reports adaptiveHwm as the constant CHUNK_HWM, never the runtime value
description: The pre-allocated _drainMsg is initialized with 'adaptiveHwm: CHUNK_HWM' (value 4) and maybePostDrain() never updates this field before calling self.postMessage. DecodeHandler correctly updates adaptiveHwm before each drain post. The scheduler reads this field to tune its push HWM.
evidence: encode-handler.ts lines 80-87: _drainMsg.adaptiveHwm = CHUNK_HWM (a constant). maybePostDrain() at lines 381-401 sets latencyMs, queueDepth, queuedBytes but never adaptiveHwm. decode-handler.ts line 404: `this._drainMsg.adaptiveHwm = hwm` (runtime value). The encode handler always reports stale constant 4 instead of the adaptive value.

title: pixelStrideForFormat does not handle 'rgb8' format and will return 4 (rgba8 default) for it
description: pixelStrideForFormat() returns stride values for 'rgba8' (4), 'rgba16' (8), and 'rgbaf32' (16) but falls through to the default '4' for any other value, including 'rgb8'.
evidence: The actual code at line 699-701 is: `if (format === "rgb8") return 3;` followed by the ternary for the other formats. The function correctly handles 'rgb8' with stride=3. The finder's code excerpt in the finding body shows the correct code but the title and description claim it does not handle 'rgb8' — a self-contradictory false alarm.

title: EncodeHandler accesses MsgEncodeStart fields via type cast to bypass missing type declarations, hiding schema drift
description: Multiple encoder options are accessed with '(this.opts as MsgEncodeStart).fieldName' casts. These fields ARE declared in MsgEncodeStart but the casts are now misleading and prevent TypeScript from catching future removals.
code_excerpt:
progressiveFlavor: (this.opts as MsgEncodeStart & { progressiveFlavor?: "dc" | "ac" }).progressiveFlavor,
progressiveDc: (this.opts as MsgEncodeStart).progressiveDc,
...
evidence: Code at lines 171-188 confirmed: 10+ fields accessed via redundant `as MsgEncodeStart` casts despite already being typed as MsgEncodeStart (line 100: `private readonly opts: MsgEncodeStart`). The comment at line 155-156 explains the cast was added when fields were 'not yet declared in the JxlModule.createEncoder interface' — but this.opts IS MsgEncodeStart, so the casts on opts are always redundant. TypeScript cannot warn on removal of these fields if they are accessed via cast.

title: wasm.createDecoder is called with progressiveDetail conditionally spread, but the JxlModule interface always includes the field as optional — the conditional spread is unnecessary and confusing
description: The null-to-absent transform at line 250 exists because opts.progressiveDetail is typed as '... | null' in MsgDecodeStart but the WASM interface uses 'undefined' absence. The real contract gap is the null/undefined impedance mismatch.
evidence: Code at line 250 confirmed. The null/undefined mismatch is real: MsgDecodeStart uses null to represent 'absent' but the WASM interface uses undefined-absence. The conditional spread is a correct workaround but the root impedance mismatch should be fixed at the type level to prevent similar workarounds from proliferating.

title: routeDecodeMessage and routeEncodeMessage silently drop messages for sessions that have already ended
description: When a decode_chunk or decode_cancel arrives after onSessionEnd() removed the handler, routeDecodeMessage silently drops the message. A cancel arriving after onSessionEnd disappears silently, and the caller may block waiting for decode_cancelled.
evidence: Code at lines 246-252 confirmed: the else branch for 'neither in decodeSessions nor pendingDecodeStarts' is absent — the message is dropped silently. For decode_cancel specifically, the scheduler sends cancel and awaits decode_cancelled; if the handler has already ended and cleaned up, no decode_cancelled is sent and the scheduler hangs. This is the described failure scenario.

title: No contract test verifying that all CodecMetric names emitted by handlers are declared in the CodecMetric union
description: No test checks that every metric name posted via postMetric() is a member of the CodecMetric discriminated union. contracts-1 shows several encode metric names are currently undeclared.
code_excerpt: // Missing test pattern: collect all metric messages, assert each name is in CodecMetric union
evidence: contracts-1 is a confirmed real bug that would be caught by this test. The test gap is genuine and the proposed test pattern is practical to implement.

title: MsgWorkerReady carries no protocol version field, so old workers and new hosts cannot detect incompatible message formats
description: MsgWorkerReady carries 'backend' and optionally 'wasmBuild' but no protocol version number. A stale cached worker will still send a valid-looking worker_ready with no mismatch detection.
evidence: Confirmed: MsgWorkerReady has no version field. In browser deployments where workers are cached independently of the main bundle, a stale worker + new host combination can silently misinterpret messages. The opportunity is genuine though the fix requires coordination across all packages.

title: postMetric allocates fresh object literals on every call, unlike the pre-allocated pattern in decode-handler
description: EncodeHandler.postMetric() creates two new objects per invocation. DecodeHandler pre-allocates _metricMsg and _metricInner and mutates them in place. The encode side allocates garbage on every metric emission.
evidence: encode-handler.ts lines 403-408 confirmed: two fresh object literals per postMetric call. decode-handler.ts lines 151-156 and 639-643 show the pre-allocated pattern. The asymmetry is real. For readEncoderChunks hot path (one postMetric per output chunk), the allocation difference is measurable under high throughput.

title: Third performance.now() call on the progress first-pixel path when two timestamps are already in scope
description: At line 483, `msg.timeToFirstPixelMs = performance.now() - this.stageStartMs` issues a third call even though `tToArray` gave us a post-copy timestamp just lines above. Using the already-captured timestamp would eliminate this call on the hot first-pixel path.
evidence: Code at lines 448-483 confirmed: t0 captured before toTransferablePixels (line 448), tToArray = performance.now()-t0 immediately after (line 450), then a third performance.now() call at line 483. Since firstPixelMetricPosted fires only once, the overhead is minimal — but the fix is trivial (use tToArray + t0 as a proxy for the post-copy absolute time).

title: onPixels allocates a new entry object on every pixel chunk even for the common no-region path
description: Every call to `onPixels` allocates `{ chunk }` or `{ chunk, region }` to push into `pixelQueue`. For a typical full-frame encode with many tiles, this creates one short-lived object per chunk.
code_excerpt:
const entry = region !== undefined ? { chunk, region } : { chunk };
    this.pixelQueue.push(entry);
evidence: Code at line 123 confirmed. A flat parallel array approach or pre-allocated pool would reduce allocations. The decode-handler avoids this by pushing raw ArrayBuffers into ChunkRing. The encode path allocates one object per tile per encode session — measurable for high-tile-count encodes.

title: maybePostDrain calls performance.now() unconditionally on every invocation, even when the guard will short-circuit
description: In EncodeHandler.maybePostDrain(), `const now = performance.now()` runs before the early-return guards. Decode-handler avoids this by accepting `now` as a parameter already captured from the post-push timestamp.
evidence: encode-handler.ts line 382: performance.now() called unconditionally. decode-handler.ts line 375: `this.maybePostDrain(now)` — now is passed as parameter already captured after push. The encode-handler calls maybePostDrain() without a parameter (no now in scope from pushPixels context), so the unconditional call is a real difference. Fix: accept now as optional parameter.

title: No throughput or allocation benchmark for the decode/encode handler hot paths
description: The test suite covers correctness but has no benchmark that measures allocation rate or message-post latency for the worker_drain / postMetric / progress hot paths.
evidence: The test file covers behavioral correctness only. The pre-allocated _metricMsg pattern in decode-handler and the allocation gap in encode-handler cannot be validated by existing tests. The opportunity is real.

title: compactQueue called after every takeNextPixels even when no compaction threshold is met
description: takeNextPixels() unconditionally calls compactQueue() after each dequeue. compactQueue() itself guards with threshold checks, so the wasted work is two comparisons per dequeue plus a function-call overhead.
evidence: Code at the takeNextPixels function (around line 307) confirmed: compactQueue() is called on both the null and non-null return paths. compactQueue() performs threshold comparison on every call. For high-tile-count encodes, inlining the threshold check to avoid the function call on the common (below-threshold) path would reduce overhead. Confirmed as a minor optimization opportunity.
